keeps. So, you know, if this was Chicago's cell therapy, everybody go, you know, Detroit's cell therapy, really? All right? Swiss cell therapy. Oh, wow. Switzerland. All right? Same pitch, a one thing pitch. It's a very powerful thing because everybody wants it. I think we can get this done and still go today. So hijack number 12. I call it the arrow to the heart. So ultimately everything boils down to four things. Self-interest. I ran this right. Almost everybody, almost all the time is actually acting out of self-interest. So if you clean up the kitchen and you're a married guy, you didn't do it just to be nice. You did it so you wouldn't get bitched at. You did it to try and buy points so you could go play golf on Saturday. You did it to try and have sex. You did it for some self-interest reason. You didn't do it to be nice. You may con yourself into, I'm just being nice, but that is not what you did because it's not what anybody does. Charity, screw this up all the time. Way too much emphasis on being a nice person and not enough emphasis on how you're going to feel because you're a nice person. Yeah, we'll give money because I'm no want to take it totally away, but there's a lot of self-interest going on. Self-esteem, it's a form of self-interest. We'd like to feel good about ourselves, not bad about ourselves, and we'd like to feel better about ourselves this afternoon than we felt when we first got up this morning. So we are always trying to do things that reinforce a positive sense of self. Then there are, for the most part, single male motives and single feelings. Female motives. Yes, I know there are exceptions. Yes, I know I am wildly generalizing. Save the notes. Okay? So on page 72, here is that. All of the stuff we manipulate ultimately boils down to either pain or pleasure, loss or gain. Take the guys first. Men are more hardwired for conquest, victory, therefore for gain than they are hardwired for fear of loss. Now that doesn't mean fear of loss doesn't work with them. It does, but by and large, we're not going to be able to do that. We're going to be able to do that. We're going to be able to do that. We're not going to be able to do that. Okay? And so why are there so many more male golfers than female golfers? It's cause women look at it and think this is stupid. All right, guys go club, kill something, small ball, come back, brag. All right, so why there's so many more male golfers than there are female golfers? Women look at it, dumbest thing I've ever seen. How do I wanna learn that? All right, so this is where you wanna play, doesn't mean you wouldn't even lead with this, but this is your greatest leverage with guys. This is why, for example, for the most part, guys don't go shop as a recreational entertaining activity. It's baked out of us. If we leave the cave, we wanna drag something back. That's simple. And so we don't, that doesn't appeal to us. So you go to the movie, you saw a movie and you now have something in your head that you saw. You left the cave, you came back with something. If you go to a mall and walk around and you come home, you got nothing. And that's not baked, well if you buy, see, and that's why we go to buy. We go, we buy something even when we went just to look. So we go over to the Bass Pro Shops, we're just gonna go hang out and we buy something because we don't wanna come home not having conquered something. Guys are much more likely to tell their conquer stories. So at the end of the day, how was your day? We tell a conquer story. I went to the post office and there was this moron in front of me and she almost killed me and she was texting and I had to drive through the ditch and around the tree and I survived. And then the clerk at the post office was a moron but I got it done and here I am home. She tells a story about the conversation she had with the clerk at the post office who has a kid who's going to college in Poland and grandma was Polish too and we exchanged recipes. Same errant, same errant. No guy comes home and tells that story. Oh, you know, I met Bertha at the post office and Bertha's Polish just like your Polish, just like your grandma and here I got a recipe. I mean, see, is that what we do? There are exceptions but not many. On the other side, women are hardwired for this. We are more external, they are more internal and they are much more about security and safety than they are about conquest. That doesn't mean they can't be, this isn't part of the mix but it is much more often your leverage is here. This is why, by the way, marketing messages aimed at both genders are more difficult to make work than if you split the genders and you can talk to them differently. All of the Glen Turner companies and all of the Tolleson companies that followed, one of the things that was done at all the opportunity meetings is you, at one point you put the women in one room and you put the men in the other and you talk to them differently because you have to talk to both of them when you have them in a room and you can't be as blunt with either one of them. So on your page 74, here's like finding the best orthodontist. So stuff I've been working on very recently. So again, self-interest, right? Okay, I want to get the best value, I don't want to overpay, I want it done right. Why do we want it done right the first time? That's self-interest. Yeah, the parents say I want it done right so the kid doesn't suffer. Really, they want it done right the first time so they don't suffer. I don't want to have to go back there 16 times to get these damn things adjusted. I got shit to do. That's what is going on in the head. That's going on more in the male head than it is in the female head, but it's going on. Right, self-esteem. This is a way to get a good parent award and assuage my guilt about whatever I'm doing that is not a good parent, that I know I'm not doing. And it gives me some bragging rights with competing parents, which by the way, you probably, if you don't have kids, you don't know this. Everybody who's got a kid is a competing parent, right? You're all competing to be a better parent than the parents that your kid plays with. Because he comes home with a story about how much better that house is than your house. And then you've got to fix it. That's what goes on all the time. So it's self-esteem issue. Now, the male, look at what is going on in his head. What's the kid to look better? Let's hear it for Dan. Good morning. All right, so we're gonna do some cold reading, but before that, one of the things he was talking about, and so I went to my course and I actually pulled out a script for you guys, for those of you who have people that come into your office or into your showroom, if you're a car dealer or something like that, what should you say? What should the people say? Anyone interested in that? Okay, so you're gonna wanna write this down. I took this right from my course. Yeah, I have a course. Psychic salesperson. Yeah, thank you. Got people shilling for me out there. Okay, so first the premise of this, then we'll get into the cold reading stuff, but I thought this was really important. When you meet somebody for the first time in any situation, whether it's on the phone or whether it's in person, even through copy, what you wanna do is you wanna set, get them to say yes a number of times immediately. So if you can get them to say yes five to seven times right away, there's actually psychological studies, studies on this that show that your conversion rate goes much higher, right? So you get them into what is called an agreement frame, right, so they're saying yes, yes, yes, yes, yes, right from the very beginning. And so that's the premise behind this. And so instead of saying, hey, can I help you, which is what's the immediate answer, no, right? What we wanna do is we always wanna align with our prospect. Okay, we wanna have an alignment with them. So the first thing to say is, are you just looking and shopping around? Right, so if I'm a salesperson, you come onto them, let's say onto my lot, right? I'm a car salesperson. Hey, are you just looking and shopping around? What's the person gonna say? Yes, now if they say no, I wanna buy a car, that's fine too, but I'm getting the yes, I'm not getting the no, and it's very disarming because they've never heard a salesperson say that before, all right, and then the next thing you would say to them is, hey, it's a great day for doing that, isn't it? And what are they gonna say? Yes, right? So first line is to align with them, right? So, hey, are you just shopping and looking around? Great, it's a great day for doing that, isn't it? Yes, so we got yes, yes, all right? The next thing is if you have any questions, just feel free to ask me. Sound good? The person says, yeah, okay, great. And then you do what I call, so that's the next line, okay? If you have any questions, just ask me, sound good? They say yes, right? And then you do what's called the Colombo strategy. And remember the movie, remember the show Colombo? Well, what did he always do? Right, yeah, he'd always like walk away, right? Like the conversation was done. So as you train your salespeople or yourself to walk away, like start to walk away, turn back and say, is this the first time you've been here? Is this the first time you've visited this shop, right? So now the person needs to answer the question. Regardless of what the question is, you then go into what the core story is of the automobile dealership, of the furniture store, of whatever it is. And now you've engaged in a conversation with them, you've developed rapport with them, you've got them to say yes a number of times, and now it changes the whole dynamic, right? So did everyone get all of those? Really, really powerful, it's simple, but the key is that the end is that Colombo strategy. You walk away, okay, yeah, there's nothing I can do, just ask me, sound good, great. Oh, by the way, is this the first time you've been in here? Let me just tell you about, right? And then you need to develop your core story, which is a whole other teaching aspect, but everyone get that? Okay, good, okay. Look how many times I got you to say yes, I'm not even selling anything, okay. All right, let's talk a little bit more about cold reading, how to read your prospect's minds. We're gonna be doing some more of the Barnum statements, and I'm gonna be going over that in a little bit more detail, but one of the first things that if you go see a psychic reader, if you wanna really mess up a psychic reader, what you do is you don't say anything, okay, you really don't give them any feedback whatsoever. By the way, there's two types of psychics. So there's the psychics that make a lot of money, and there's the psychics that don't. And the psychics that make a lot of money are using a lot of these techniques called cold reading, all right, and by the way, cold reading is really a series of techniques, it's not all generalized statements, it's a series of techniques, but the really good ones are following a system, right. Then there's what are called shut eyes, which people who really believe that they are psychic, okay. So you're leaving, you're blowing it, man, you're blowing it, okay, all right, all right. So, it's always fun, okay. So one of the first things that a psychic needs to do, a good one, they follow a strategy, the first thing is to encourage cooperation, right, encourage cooperation, and when we're selling, we want to also encourage cooperation, right. And so here is an actual script for encouraging cooperation, all right, I'm gonna have to go over here and read it, says, okay, so we'll use a financial advisor, but this is really a fill-in-the-blank script. Now if I was a psychic, I would be saying different things, but this is a great way, after you've done some small talk and you've developed rapport and everything like that, this is a great script to use. Mr. Smith, I've been helping people reach their financial goals for over 35 years. So obviously I'm experienced, all right. So let's just analyze that one little sentence right there, because it seems very innocuous, but it's really powerful. So the word obviously is extremely powerful. Whenever you say the word, it's called an LI word, whenever you say an LI word like obviously or naturally, what tends to happen is whatever is said afterwards is accepted, all right, so, because it's just hardwired into us. If we use obviously or naturally, what we say, whatever comes after that's accepted, all right. All right, so obviously I'm experienced, and of course I prepared for our meeting by doing some homework regarding your situation, but as you know, okay, so as you know, is an assumptive statement, and you can use as you know in a lot of different situations, okay, as you know, as you are aware. Now even if they're not, again, what you say after that, they tend to have, they tend to agree with, all right. So as you know, every person is different, every person has different goals, outcomes, desires that they want to achieve and fulfill. So obviously there is again, I don't want to make suggestions about a plan of action before, and I don't want to make suggestions about a plan of action before finding more out about you and about what your financial goals are, okay. That makes sense, doesn't it, right? And so right there, by then, now they're gonna answer your questions, right, and now you're gonna go into a series of questions to elicit their values, find out what they truly desire, but if you don't use this before going into trying to elicit their values, a lot of people will hold back and not give information. So that's why a psychic would use a variation of that script so that the person will then start giving them information which they can then feed back later in the reading. We're doing it so we can elicit their values, get them to talk and find out what they want and then structure our sales presentation around that, around what they told us that they want. Does that make sense, everybody? Yes, okay. So the Barnum statements, there's actually 12 Barnum statements. Dan gave you some of them. And it's actually called the foyer effect, if you wanna look it up, F-O-Y-E-R, foyer effect. And there was a professor who said he was gonna do, said that he did a personality analysis on his class, on each individual member in the class. And he gave everybody an envelope inside the envelope with the scientific personality analysis. And he had everybody read it to themselves and he asked how accurate was this? And he said how many people had 80%, 90% accuracy, 95%, almost everybody's hands went up. Then he revealed to them that it was all of the same. So all of the personality readings were exactly the same. So again, people have a tendency to figure out or associate with what you say, try to figure out a way to make it work for themselves. But here's what's really interesting, which a lot of people don't know. Later on they did an experiment where they did the complete opposite of those foyer statements, so those Barnum statements. The complete opposite, did the same experiment and again, 80 to 85% of the people said, yes, it was 80, people said it was 80 to 85% accurate. So really cool. So here is a really good one. And I'm gonna share, the other thing that Dan didn't talk about and why this makes so powerful either in one to one selling or one to many selling, such as in copywriting, is that you're also developing rapport. You're developing a real level of rapport because when you use these statements, the person who you're using them, whether they're reading it or whether you're talking to them or whether it's on video, is saying, hey, this person really understands me. They understand me. If you feel like somebody really understands you, don't you feel immediately closer to them, like them and trust them? Yes, right? Okay, so you pride yourself on being an independent thinker and don't accept other people's opinions without satisfactory proof. Now, where do you go with that? So that could be in copy or that could be when you say it. Now, this is a perfect, the person's gonna say yes. So then you go and you move into your testimonials and your social proof and all of that. Here's what's powerful about that though. So it's a perfect lead in, you're using cold reading to lead in to showing testimonials. But here's what's exciting. You pride yourself on being an independent thinker and don't accept other people's opinions without satisfactory proof. They say yes to that. Therefore, it carries, because we wanna be congruent with ourselves in what we say, it carries that if we give them satisfactory proof, right, they will accept that what you're saying is true. You guys following that? I mean, it's really, really powerful. So it's more than, so look what's happened. Number one, yeah, the person says, yeah, this guy understands me. That's exactly right, that's exactly who I am. And then when you get that person to agree that, yeah, if I get satisfactory proof, I'm in, that's what happens with that statement. Another Barnum statement. You prefer a certain amount of change and variety and become dissatisfied when hemmed in by restrictions and limitations. This is really, really powerful to use if what you're doing is something that's a little bit different, right? Or it's a little bit off, something that they're not used to doing. So again, if they agree to that, and who's not gonna agree to that, right? If they agree to that, it also carries that you can show them something. By the way, this stuff all works when I was, oh, I shouldn't tell you that, okay, but, all right. When I was dating, all right, this stuff works like magic, right? Because dating is selling, right? Huh? Easy there, easy there, killer. Okay, all right. Now here's a really great one. All right, so this one combines a whole bunch of stuff. The cold reading line is the one in red. At times you've had serious doubts about whether you've done the right thing or made the right decisions. Absolutely, all right? So let's, I'm just using this as a financial advisor example. Of course, everyone has had their ups and downs and struggles financially at times. That's only to be expected. But if you implement this plan now, your future looks very bright. So this is a Barnum statement combined with something else called predicting the future. All right, so one of the things that psych, you go to a psychic, right? Because they want to predict the future. Dan talked about future predictions. But it's even more powerful than that because there's a whole bunch of stuff going on here. The first thing is at times you have serious doubts. That whole statement, again, is the report building. Yes, that's me, he understands me. The next thing is saying, hey, it's okay. You've had ups and downs, but there's also something called an embedded, so we're also predicting the future. But in that little statement, we're also, there's also an embedded command. The embedded command is implement this plan now. Right, so the guy is saying yes to that, right? So it would read, by the way, we don't have time to go to this in detail, but embedded commands, even in copy, are really powerful. Now verbally, it would sound like you struggle financially at times, that's only to be expected. But if you implement this plan now, your future will look very bright. So there's a slight pause. Now by the way, it doesn't read like this. That's only to be expected, but if you implement this plan now, right, that's no good. Your future looks very bright. In copy the way I do it, is I break it apart. Like I use ellipses, or I use dashes, right? So I break it apart like that in copy. I'll underline it, I'll highlight it. So you can put in an embedded command, and if you read Dan's stuff and you read my stuff, there's a ton of embedded commands inside the copy. Does everyone want to follow me in this? Okay, all right. Another type of cold reading, I'm just cranking these out really fast so we can get through them all.
